intro: Midjourney just made the wildest pivot in tech — from AI-generated art to full-body ultrasound scanners that aim to rival MRI quality, complete with plans for a San Francisco spa where you can get scanned. It's one of those stories that makes you do a double-take, and it's far from the only headline worth your attention today. We've also got a massive breach exposing credentials for half of all internet-facing Fortinet firewalls, Tesco suing Broadcom over eye-watering VMware price hikes, and a growing reality check on whether enterprises are actually getting ROI from their AI spending.

stories.title: v2.1.181
stories.summary: Version 2.1.181 of Claude Code added a `/config key=value` syntax for setting configurations from the prompt, an `sandbox.allowAppleEvents` opt-in setting for macOS sandboxed commands, a `CLAUDE_CLIENT_PRESENCE_FILE` environment variable to suppress mobile push notifications when the user is present, upgraded the bundled Bun runtime to 1.4, and improved streaming of long paragraphs so text appears line by line.
stories.opinion: This is a solid incremental update with practical quality-of-life improvements. The `/config` command syntax is particularly useful for quickly adjusting settings without leaving the workflow. The presence file feature for suppressing push notifications shows thoughtful attention to the developer experience in remote/mobile scenarios. The Bun runtime upgrade and streaming improvements are welcome maintenance and UX enhancements.

stories.title: Midjourney goes from generating cat images to full-body ultrasound scans
stories.summary: Midjourney, the AI image generation company, has unveiled its first hardware product: a full-body ultrasound scanner developed in partnership with Butterfly Network. Called The Midjourney Scanner, it uses a ring of 40 ultrasound-on-chip imaging modules to capture vertical slices of the body, analyzing muscle, fat, bone, and organs. CEO David Holz claims the device aims for image quality comparable to MRI and envisions it being used for daily or annual body composition tracking. The company also plans to build a San Francisco spa where people can experience the scanning technology, with the broader goal of bringing preventative full-body scanning to billions of people.
stories.opinion: This is a fascinating and unexpected pivot for Midjourney, moving from generative AI art into medical imaging hardware. The ambition is impressive — democratizing body scanning could genuinely transform preventative healthcare if the image quality claims hold up. However, there are significant reasons for skepticism. Ultrasound being 'comparable to MRI' is an extraordinary claim that needs rigorous clinical validation, not just a CEO demo. The spa framing feels very Silicon Valley wellness-cult…

stories.title: How to turn off AI in your Google Docs
stories.summary: The article provides step-by-step instructions for disabling AI features, specifically Gemini pop-ups, in Google Docs. The author describes two methods: first, clicking 'Gemini' in the top menu bar and selecting 'bottom bar preferences' to turn off the bottom bar AI box; second, a more comprehensive approach through Gmail Settings where users can navigate to 'Google Workspace smart features' and toggle off smart features to disable Gemini pop-ups across Google Workspace. The author humorously recounts trying to ask Gemini itself how to remove it, only for the AI to suggest clicking an 'X' that merely closed the conversation rather than removing the feature.
stories.opinion: This is a genuinely useful and well-written how-to article that addresses a real user frustration with increasingly aggressive AI integration in productivity tools. The author's voice is refreshingly honest and funny — the detail about asking Gemini to help remove itself and it exhibiting a 'survival instinct' is both amusing and illustrative of a broader problem with AI features that are easy to deploy but hard to dismiss. The Benjamin Franklin quip is delightful. The article highlights a legi…

stories.title: Roelof Botha joins SpaceX’s board of directors
stories.summary: Roelof Botha, former Sequoia Capital managing partner, has been appointed to SpaceX's board of directors to fill an existing vacancy, just days after SpaceX went public in the largest IPO ever. Botha will also join the board's audit committee. He has a long history with Elon Musk, having been recruited by Musk to PayPal in 2000. Botha left Sequoia late last year amid controversy at the firm. SpaceX disclosed that a family member of Botha's has worked at the company since January 2025. The appointment brings SpaceX's board to nine directors, though Musk retains over 80% voting power and control over board composition. Sequoia invested in SpaceX in 2019 and reportedly held a 1.5% stake worth over $20 billion heading into the IPO.
stories.opinion: This appointment appears to be a strategic move to add financial credibility and public company governance experience to SpaceX's newly public board, though it raises questions about true board independence given Botha's 25-year personal relationship with Musk and Musk's overwhelming control of the company. The family member employment disclosure, while transparent, adds another layer of potential conflict of interest. For investors hoping for meaningful board oversight of Musk, Botha's appoint…

stories.title: After unveiling ridiculously expensive AR glasses, Snap’s stock takes a dive
stories.summary: Snap's stock dropped over 5% following the launch of its long-awaited AR glasses, Specs, which will retail at nearly $2,200. Analysts and observers questioned the profitability path given that Snap's core demographic — teenagers — are unlikely to afford such a price. CEO Evan Spiegel defended the pricing by comparing Specs to high-end computers and positioning them between Meta's Ray-Bans and Apple Vision Pro. Snap's stock had already declined 30% over the past year before this announcement.
stories.opinion: This feels like a significant strategic misstep by Snap. Pricing AR glasses at $2,200 for a company whose primary user base is teenagers seems fundamentally disconnected from market reality. Spiegel's comparison to high-end laptops is a stretch — laptops are established productivity tools with clear utility, while consumer AR glasses remain an unproven category. The stock decline reflects legitimate investor concern that Snap spent over a decade developing a product without a clear path to mass…

stories.title: NEA’s Tiffany Luck says enterprises are still figuring out their AI ROI
stories.summary: NEA partner Tiffany Luck discusses the growing tension between AI hype and actual enterprise ROI on TechCrunch's Equity podcast. The article highlights the trend of 'tokenmaxxing,' where companies aggressively pushed AI usage, only to face budget overruns—Uber reportedly exhausted its annual AI budget in months, some companies cut Claude licenses, and Meta killed its internal AI leaderboard. Luck, who previously helped companies adopt e-commerce, now focuses on AI investments, particularly 'magic moments' in consumer business. The podcast covers personal agents, AI IPOs, and how startups are emerging to help enterprises track return on AI spend.
stories.opinion: This article captures a critical and increasingly relevant moment in the AI adoption cycle: the hangover after the initial spending binge. The concept of 'tokenmaxxing' is a vivid illustration of how companies rushed into AI without proper cost controls or ROI frameworks. The specific examples—Uber's budget blowout, Claude license cuts, Meta's leaderboard shutdown—provide concrete evidence that the enterprise AI market is entering a more sober, accountability-driven phase. This is actually heal…

stories.title: Massive breach spills credentials for thousands of sensitive networks
stories.summary: Security researchers have uncovered a massive breach of nearly 74,000 Fortinet firewall devices across 194 countries, exposing plaintext credentials for major organizations including Oracle, Chevron, Lenovo, FedEx, a NATO defense contractor, and Fortinet itself. Russian-speaking, criminally motivated threat actors mass-scanned the internet for FortiGate remote login endpoints, then used a custom binary with 25,000 threads to spray hundreds of thousands of endpoints with credential combinations. Successful logins gave attackers network access, after which they intercepted SSL VPN authentication hashes and cracked them using a dedicated 45-GPU cluster managed via Hashtopolis, employing a 12-level recursive password cracking system. The compromised devices represent roughly half of all internet-facing Fortinet firewalls. Attackers moved laterally to compromise Active Directory and centrali…
stories.opinion: This breach is staggering in its scope and represents a catastrophic failure in network security hygiene at a global scale. The fact that roughly half of all internet-facing Fortinet firewalls were compromised suggests that a huge number of organizations are running these critical security devices with weak or default credentials, which is inexcusable for enterprise-grade infrastructure. The attackers' methodology — while technically impressive with the 45-GPU cracking cluster and recursive pas…

stories.title: Tesco moving 40,000 server workloads off VMware amid Broadcom's “abusive conduct”
stories.summary: Tesco, the UK retail giant, is migrating 40,000 server workloads off VMware after Broadcom allegedly hiked prices by approximately 175 percent following its acquisition of VMware. In UK High Court filings, Tesco accuses Broadcom of 'abusive conduct,' claiming Broadcom refused to honor Tesco's existing perpetual licenses purchased in 2021 and instead demanded new subscription-based licenses at inflated prices. Broadcom also stopped supporting Tesco's VMware products in January 2025. Tesco expects the migration to take until at least the end of 2027, noting significant operational risks and costs. The company is seeking at least 100 million pounds in damages each from Broadcom, VMware, and reseller Computacenter. Tesco rejected four offers from Broadcom, with one charging $23.5 million annually — which Tesco called 'manifestly unfair and excessive.' Broadcom has denied the price hikes wer…
stories.opinion: This case exemplifies the worst fears that VMware customers had when Broadcom completed its acquisition. Refusing to honor existing perpetual licenses and forcing customers into dramatically more expensive subscription bundles is a textbook example of leveraging vendor lock-in for extraction. A 175 percent price increase with no corresponding increase in value is hard to defend on any grounds other than monopolistic pricing power. Tesco's situation — forced into a costly, risky multi-year migra…

tweet_headlines.author: cursor_ai
tweet_headlines.headline: Cursor now lets you __move local agents to the cloud__, prompt from your phone, run many in parallel, and get back PRs with demos of their work.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
It’s now easier to move local agents to the cloud so they can keep working with your laptop closed.

Prompt Cursor from your phone, run many agents in parallel, and get back PRs with demos of their work. https://t.co/vuh5aZbH3e

tweet_headlines.author: sairahul1
tweet_headlines.headline: Anthropic quietly published a __context engineering template__ that's getting praise from developers as a solid starting point for agent design.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
This is actually a really solid context engineering template.

Kudos, @AnthropicAI https://t.co/lQjNghcqTW https://t.co/JrGU5c6xKn

tweet_headlines.author: danshipper
tweet_headlines.headline: Agents created __17 million pull requests on GitHub__ in March alone — Dan Shipper sat down with GitHub's COO to talk about what that means for code.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
GitHub has a front-row seat to how code is changing now that everyone—and their army of agents—can ship code. In March alone, agents created 17 million pull requests on the platform.

That’s why I was thrilled @hammer_mt was on hand to interview @github COO @kdaigle at Microsoft https://t.co/bwMYCgLOW1

tweet_headlines.author: mattpocockuk
tweet_headlines.headline: Matt Pocock ships v1 of his open-source skills repo with a __63% reduction in token cost__ and new model-invocable vs. user-invocable skill types.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
Announcing mattpocock/skills v1

- Achieved a 63% reduction in token cost for skill descriptions
- Split skills into model-invocable and user-invocable skills, adding /codebase-design, /domain-modeling, and /grilling
- (UPDATED) /writing-great-skills - rewritten from the ground https://t.co/JVLhplTwID

tweet_headlines.author: 9to5mac
tweet_headlines.headline: iOS 27 contains __colorful new Siri animation assets__ — including "listening" and "thinking" states — that Apple hasn't shown off yet.
tweet_headlines.source_text: iOS 27 contains a set of colorful Siri animation assets, including "listening" and "thinking" states, that we haven't seen yet. https://t.co/pLHH9MYqT3

tweet_headlines.author: urdesignfriend
tweet_headlines.headline: A designer built __Espresso Terminal__, a terminal with built-in Apple Intelligence that lets you run commands in plain language instead of CLI syntax.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
Several weeks back, I built a terminal meant for designers. 

Espresso Terminal has built-in Apple intelligence to do commands in plain language, versus traditional commands. https://t.co/9cBjmqtQnW
